name: i18n-sync-translations
description: Automatically detect missing i18n translation keys and sync them across all language files using the Cursor CLI. Supports JSON and Java .properties formats.

enumDescriptions: RECOMMENDED - Gemini 3 Flash (Google) - Best for translations: fast, cheap, strongest multilingual training data
Gemini 3 Pro (Google) - Higher quality multilingual, slower than Flash. Use for nuanced business language
Claude 4.5 Sonnet (Anthropic) - Good general model, decent multilingual
Claude 4.5 Sonnet Thinking (Anthropic) - Overkill for translations
Claude 4.6 Opus (Anthropic) - Overkill for translations, expensive
Claude 4.6 Opus Thinking (Anthropic) - Overkill for translations, expensive and slow
GPT-5.3 Codex (OpenAI) - Code-optimized, not ideal for translations
Auto - Let Cursor pick (may not choose the best model for translations)
i18nSync.model.markdownDescription: LLM model to use for translations. **Gemini 3 Flash is strongly recommended** - it has the best multilingual training data (Google), is fast, and cost-effective. Gemini 3 Pro is the runner-up for higher quality on nuanced text.

i18nSync.contextWindowSize.description: Number of adjacent sibling keys (before and after) to include as translation context. These existing translations help the LLM understand the domain and tone. Set to 0 to disable context inference.

i18nSync.batchSize.description: Number of translation keys to send per batch to the Cursor CLI.

i18nSync.concurrentLimit.description: Number of concurrent translation batches to process simultaneously. Bumped from 2 to 3 in v1.3.2 — the bottleneck is per-call CLI/backend latency, so additional parallelism amortises wait time across languages. Drop back to 1-2 if you start hitting rate limits.

i18nSync.maxRetries.description: Maximum number of retry attempts for failed translation batches.

i18nSync.translationTone.description: The tone/style to use for translations (e.g., 'formal business', 'casual', 'technical').

i18nSync.cursorCliPath.markdownDescription:
Path or name of the Cursor CLI executable.

- `auto` *(default)* — auto-detect: prefers the new `agent` binary, falls back to the legacy `cursor` binary.
- `agent` — force the **new** Cursor CLI (renamed in 2026, invoked as `agent --print ...`).
- `cursor` — force the **legacy** CLI (invoked as `cursor agent --print ...`).
- An absolute path — point at a custom install. The basename decides whether the `agent` subcommand is used (`.../agent` ⇒ no subcommand, anything else ⇒ legacy mode).

i18nSync.cliTimeoutSeconds.markdownDescription: How long (seconds) to wait for a single Cursor CLI batch before giving up and retrying. Bumped from 90 to 180 in v1.3.2 — the dominant cost is Cursor CLI/model backend latency, which can easily exceed 60-90s for a single call even with a small prompt. On timeout, accumulated stdout/stderr is dumped to the output channel so you can see what (if anything) the CLI produced.

i18nSync.autoSync.markdownDescription: **Background auto-sync.** When enabled, watches `i18n-en.json` / `Messages.properties` for changes and automatically translates missing keys in the background every few minutes. No prompts, no popups - just silently keeps translations in sync.

i18nSync.autoSyncIntervalMinutes.markdownDescription: How often (in minutes) the auto-sync checks for missing translations after detecting a file change. The timer resets on each save, so rapid edits are debounced.

i18nSync.debugMode.markdownDescription:
**Verbose debug logging.** Enable this when a sync seems stuck or batches keep timing out. With debug mode on, the output channel additionally shows:

- The exact `agent`/`cursor` command and PID for each batch.
- Live `stdout`/`stderr` chunks as the CLI streams them (so you see *immediately* if the CLI is producing output or just hanging).
- A heartbeat every 15s while a batch is running.
- The full prompt sent to the model and the elapsed time on completion.

Partial stdout/stderr is *always* dumped on a timeout, regardless of this setting.
